sessions.details: recorded 1970-08-19 for Transcription Service "Top of the Pops"
recorded 1977-05-02 for John Peel. First TX 1977-05-10
recorded 1979-10-31 for Friday Rock Show. First TX 1979-11-09

track_listing.track: 'If I Could Do It All Over Again I'd Do It All Over You'

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. Thomas the Vance here. TV on the radio. Welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
And tonight our archive sessions are by Caravan. Two sets from Caravan. One from 1970 and the last one they did for Radio 1 ever in 1977.
And we have the first ever session from a new London band whose name is Praying Mantis.
We've got classic cuts tonight by Deep Purple, Armaldool 2, Armandool 2, that should read,
Black Sabbath, and new stuff by Styx, Pink Floyd, Stingray, and Wild Horses.
And don't forget our Friday Night Connection, which tonight is rather short, so don't blink, you might miss it.
But this evening we start with the Rolling Stones.
With a track on their new album called Cornerstone.

first of three tracks tonight.

time for the Friday Night Connection,
and we're going to send our £10 record token this week

I can see the smile on your face from here.

Trampled Underfoot by Led Zeppelin,
Wooden Ships by Jefferson Airplane,
and Breadfan by Budgie.
Surprisingly enough, not a lot of response for that at all,
and even some wrong ones as well.
The answer, of course, was flight.
And Peter Rogers, 17, Sunningdale, Oma, in County Tyrone,
you get yourself a £10 record token.
Now, Peter has put together a rather tricky and quite clever
and very, for us anyway, short segment of music
for the Friday Night Connection.
Have a listen. It's a trick.
...practising their craft anymore.
By the year 2006, the music known today as the blues

of your local public library.
So tonight, ladies and gentlemen, while we still can,
let us welcome from Rock Island, Illinois,
the blues band of Jell-O and Jake

To bury my body, a dog needs a shelter.

You crafty devil.

You well, well have earned your £10 record token.

then there is definitely one in there.
Down on a postcard, you should jot it, or in a letter, any form of communication you like.

Three bits of music that have a connection, some sort of interwoven meaning, or whatever.
and send it to The Friday Night Connection,

And we'll be doing that connection

not next week, but the week after.
Of course, don't forget, next week is our first birthday.
I feel all of one year of age now.
And the whole programme is based on all the records that you've sent in.
We asked you to send in your top three album tracks of all time,
which many of you did, and we thank you very much for that.
We've put them all through a computer and various things like that.
And the whole two-hour programme
is going to be based entirely on your choice of music.
Now, it's going to be a two-hour programme
of music reflecting exactly what you want to hear,
what you absolutely like, with very little speech in it.
We're going to try and weave it together musically
so that it really does work.
Right, it's 11.14, and now here's track number two,
from Praying Mantis.
It's a good band. I've never heard of them before.
The album's just come out on Carrera, C-A-R-R-E-R-E.
The band are called Stingray. They're from South Africa.
And the track there is entitled Better The Devil You Know.
And prior to that, Praying Mantis and Lovers To The Grave.
Right, now let's go back to the 2nd of the 5th, 1977.
Now, this was the last session that was done by Caravan for BBC Radio 1.
Peeler put it out, and it's good.
We're in the studio on the 2nd of the 5th in 1977.
The tracks you heard were Nightmare, Behind You, The Last Unicorn and Better By Far.
And really good tracks they were as well.
And that was the last time they were in a BBC studio.
We were phoning around today to try and find out what was happening to Caravan.
I phoned the record companies and nobody could come up with a positive answer with regard to a record deal.
But a good friend of ours called Clive, who's just through the window here in the studios,
tells us that Caravan are touring England at the moment with what is almost the original line-up.
That's everybody except the bassist and the singer, Richard Sinclair,
who's actually living in Canterbury and busy organising his own band there.
It's called RSVP.
Dec Messica is playing bass with them at the moment.
The Reformed Canavan play their hometown of Canterbury next Tuesday
and they're hoping to release an album when they can get a deal of live material
from their month-long reunion tour.
And it seems that Richard Sinclair actually backed out
because the band wanted to play older material and he wanted to play new material.
Well, good luck to him anyway with RSVP.
And that session was specially for Geoffrey Smith
of Seven Oaks in Kent, who loves it.
Real classic, Black Sabbath and Paranoid,
specially for Patrick Goodwin from his mum, Stella Goodwin,
because they listen to the programme together quite a lot.
And before that, you heard the last number tonight by Praying Mantis,
and it was called Johnny Cool, and so indeed was the set.
...James Harvest, and a track from their just-released album,
which is out on the Polydor label as always.
The album's called Eyes of the Universe,
and the track was Berratos.
Many thanks to the four gentlemen in Prey Mantis
for their first ever broadcast on the radio.
Nice one, gentlemen.
Thank you, Peter, Tino, Chris, and Chris.
Fila and Mike Reed.
Next week, we'll be flying the flag for good music.
Let's get rock on tomorrow.
Four o'clock, I'll be there with a sore throat,
but I'll be doing my best.
Michael Palin's on the show.
Should be good for a laugh.
6.30 tomorrow on Radio 1.
Climax Blues Band in concert.
And next week, we have Now We Are One.
Two hours of rock music, all chosen by you.

Thank you, Clive.

Don't miss next week.
Oh, very fluent.
See you tomorrow at four o'clock. I'm off for a hot tolly.
Have a good night. Good night.
It's midnight and we're together on all wavelengths,
And this is Nick Jackson in the Midnight Newsroom.
Firstly, the headlines.
British soldiers pack their kit for Zimbabwe, Rhodesia.
The UN Security Council tells the embassy mob in Iran
to release their hostages.
Three men are found guilty of murdering Carl, the newspaper boy.
Hundreds of British soldiers are standing by to go to Zimbabwe, Rhodesia.
If there's agreement at the Lancaster House Conference,
their job will be to serve in a Commonwealth force
to monitor the ceasefire during the transitional period
to full independence.
The conference itself will be resuming this morning
with the Patriotic Front guerrillas replying to the British proposals
on what should happen during that transitional period.
Our report on how things stand
is by our diplomatic correspondent, Angus McDermott.
The Front have welcomed the British decision
to assemble a Commonwealth ceasefire monitoring force
and no doubt regard this as an important concession
towards meeting their demands for a United Nations force.
The biggest point remaining at issue
is the length of the interim period.
The Front take the view that the short British period
of two months after the ceasefire
gives the Bishop Muzarewa group an unfair electoral advantage.
The British spokesman, Mr Fenn,
described the France document as disappointing
but not a matter for despair.
As for the Commonwealth Ceasefire Monitoring Force,
Mr Fenn said that a number of governments
had been asked to provide contingents
and a number had agreed.
It's believed that a total force of some hundreds,
probably experts in the field of communications,
will be needed, they'll in no way constitute a fighting force.
The question of acceptability of all the contingents to all parties
at the conference is a delicate one.
Delegations would have to be consulted to see if some nationalities
might be unacceptable in what's described as a task of delicacy and importance,
and this might rule out the use of personnel from some African countries.
The United Nations Security Council has made a strong appeal
to the authorities in Iran to release the 60 American hostages
being held in the United States Embassy in Tehran.
The call came after an appeal from Washington
for help in resolving the matter.
A report from the UN by Brian Saxton.
After private consultations, which lasted more than two hours,
the Council then authorised its president,
Mr Sergio Palacios de Vizio of Bolivia,
to issue a statement expressing its profound concern
over the prolonged detention of American diplomatic personnel in Iran.
The President said that while not wishing to interfere
in the internal affairs of any country,
he wanted to emphasise that the principle of the inviolability
of diplomatic personnel and establishment
should be respected in all cases
in accordance with internationally accepted norms.
Farmworkers in England and Wales
are to get pay rises averaging 21% next year.
The increase was agreed at the Agricultural Wages Board,
in spite of opposition from the National Farmers Union,
which said that some farmers would have no choice
but to cut staff.
At Stafford Crown Court, three men have been found guilty
of murdering the 13-year-old newspaper boy, Carl Bridgewater.
They were James Robinson, Vincent Hickey,
and his cousin, Michael Hickey.
The fourth defendant, Patrick Malloy, was found guilty of manslaughter.
All four were convicted on a further charge of burglary
at the farm where Carl was found dead last September.
The judge will pass sentence on Monday.
A report from Graham McLagan.
When the judge spoke to the jury of the murder,
he said any normal person couldn't help but be moved
by feelings of emotion, by feelings of deepest sympathy
and by feelings of anger and disgust against the killers.
The public revulsion at the killing was such
that many people donated money towards a reward
for information on the killers.
Criminals also helped, and one of them gave the original information
that Vincent Hickey might have done the murder.
As a result, the police actually questioned Hickey,
but let him go as he seemed to have a good alibi.
It was only when he was in custody on another matter,
several weeks later, that the police got their breakthrough.
In an attempt to get bail, Hickey started telling the police
that he knew something about the murder.
That information led the police on to the other defendants.
But even now, no-one other than the defendants
knows who pulled the trigger.
The filming of a BBC panorama team
of the so-called IRA takeover of a Northern Ireland village
has now been referred to the Director of Public Prosecutions.
The Attorney-General, Sir Michael Havers,
has asked the Director, Sir Thomas Hetherington,
to open inquiries.
Sir Thomas, in turn, is asking Commander Peter Duffy,
the head of Scotland Yard's anti-terrorist squad,
to carry out the investigation.
Sir Thomas says there will be full cooperation and liaison
with the Royal Ulster Constabulary.
The British Steel Corporation has confirmed
that the Shotten Works in North Wales
are to be closed down by March next year,
throwing nearly 6,500 people out of work.
And it also says that the Cleveland Works is...
